title: Reading pig behaviour
subtitle: What pig posture, vocalisations, and social dynamics tell you about health and stress.
excerpt: Pigs communicate a great deal through posture, movement, and sound. A pig keeper who knows the normal patterns recognises abnormal ones quickly. The daily observation habit that catches health problems early is built on knowing what a content pig looks like and how it differs from one that is off, stressed, or injured.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Pigs (2003), gov.uk. RSPCA: pig welfare needs. Brambell Committee: welfare of intensively kept animals (ethology section). Mendl, Burman, Paul (2010): Pig cognition and affect, Applied Animal Behaviour Science.

glossaryTerms.definition: The natural foraging behaviour of pigs using the nose disc (snout) and neck muscles to dig into and turn over soil, leaf litter, and other substrates in search of food. A strong rooting drive is a welfare indicator of a normal healthy pig; pigs deprived of rooting opportunity redirect the behaviour onto pen-mates (bar-biting, flank-nosing, belly-nosing) causing injury.

A damaging behaviour in pigs where one pig repeatedly bites the tail of another, initiating a wound that other pigs then escalate. Often starts as a redirected exploration or foraging behaviour in animals with insufficient space, enrichment, or feed provision. Once a wound is visible and bleeding, affected pigs must be separated immediately as the group will intensify biting.

Pigs cool themselves by wallowing in water or mud. Unlike most mammals, pigs have very few sweat glands and cannot regulate body temperature effectively through sweating. A pig without access to shade or wallowing in hot weather will show rapid-onset heat stress: panting, drooling, sprawling flat on the ground. Outdoor pigs in summer need a wallow or wet area and shade.

content.text: A healthy pig in good conditions is active, curious, and vocal at feeding time. It rests in a loose group with pen-mates, rises readily when approached, and shows a normal level of nosing,
, and exploring. Deviations from this baseline are where daily observation earns its value.

A resting pig lies on its side or chest, usually in contact with at least one other pig. The group typically rests together in a heap in cool weather and spreads out in warm weather. A pig lying apart from the group, especially one that remains lying when others rise at feeding time, is an alert sign. Press firmly on its flanks and note whether it grunts or groans in pain as it moves.

Content pigs grunt steadily during rooting and feeding. A pig giving short, sharp squeals when moved or handled may be in pain. Sustained, loud screaming during normal handling indicates high fear, not necessarily pain. A pig that is silent and unresponsive when the rest of the group is active and vocalising is unwell until proven otherwise.
Pigs pile together in cold conditions and spread out and seek shade or

in hot conditions. A group sleeping piled in one corner in summer, with the pen wet from drinker overflow, is probably cold or in a draught. A pig lying flat on its side with legs extended, panting in warm weather, is heat-stressed. Panting in a pig is serious; provide water, shade, and wet the skin if the temperature is above 25°C.

is often a pig with a slightly wet or reddened tail rather than a visible wound. Check tails at every visit during the growing period. Once tail biting escalates to bleeding, the victim must be separated and the bite wounds treated. Look at the underlying cause: insufficient space, feed competition at a narrow trough, lack of rooting substrate, or stocking density.
